traditions.name: The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints

keyFigures.name: Joseph Smith Jr.
keyFigures.role: Founding prophet, translator of the Book of Mormon

keyFigures.name: Brigham Young
keyFigures.role: Second president, led migration to Utah

keyFigures.name: Oliver Cowdery
keyFigures.role: Scribe, witness to the Book of Mormon plates

keyFigures.name: Sidney Rigdon
keyFigures.role: Early leader and theologian

keyConcepts: Book of Mormon as restored scripture
Plan of Salvation and celestial kingdoms
traditions.evidence: Restorationist Christian movement founded by Joseph Smith, claiming restoration of original Christianity through new scripture and prophetic authority. Approximately 17 million members worldwide.

traditions.name: Jehovah's Witnesses
traditions.originDateLabel: Bible Student movement from 1870s, incorporated as Watch Tower 1881

keyFigures.name: Charles Taze Russell
keyFigures.role: Founder of the Bible Student movement and Watch Tower Society

keyFigures.name: Joseph Franklin Rutherford
keyFigures.role: Second president, adopted 'Jehovah's Witnesses' name in 1931

keyFigures.name: Nathan Homer Knorr
keyFigures.role: Third president, expanded global missionary work

keyFigures.name: Frederick William Franz
keyFigures.role: Fourth president, principal Bible scholar

keyConcepts: Jehovah as God's exclusive personal name
Christ as God's first creation (non-Trinitarian Christology)
Kingdom of God as literal heavenly government
Refusal of blood transfusions and military service
traditions.evidence: Millenarian restorationist Christian denomination with distinct non-Trinitarian theology. Approximately 8.7 million active publishers worldwide, organized through the Watch Tower Bible and Tract Society.

traditions.name: Ahmadiyya Islam

keyFigures.name: Mirza Ghulam Ahmad
keyFigures.role: Founder, claimed to be the promised Mahdi and Messiah

keyFigures.name: Hakim Noor-ud-Din
keyFigures.role: First Khalifatul Masih (successor-caliph)

keyFigures.name: Mirza Bashir-ud-Din Mahmud Ahmad
keyFigures.role: Second Khalifa, expanded international missions

keyFigures.name: Mirza Masroor Ahmad
keyFigures.role: Fifth and current Khalifatul Masih (since 2003)

keyConcepts: Continuation of prophethood subordinate to Muhammad
Jesus survived crucifixion and died naturally in Kashmir
Jihad of the pen (intellectual struggle, not armed)
Love for All, Hatred for None (motto)
traditions.evidence: Reformist Islamic movement claiming fulfillment of Islamic eschatological prophecies. Approximately 10-20 million adherents in over 200 countries. Considered non-Muslim by mainstream Islamic authorities due to claims regarding prophethood.

traditions.name: Tenrikyo

keyFigures.name: Nakayama Miki (Oyasama)
keyFigures.role: Foundress, Shrine of God the Parent

keyFigures.name: Iburi Izo
keyFigures.role: First Honseki (intermediary of divine direction after Miki)

keyFigures.name: Nakayama Shinnosuke
keyFigures.role: Miki's husband, early supporter of the movement

keyFigures.name: Tenri-O-no-Mikoto
keyFigures.role: God the Parent, deity who revealed truth through Miki

keyConcepts: Kashimono-karimono (the body as a thing lent by God)
The Kanrodai (the Jiba as center of creation)
Eight dusts of the mind (greed, miserliness, hatred, etc.)
traditions.evidence: Japanese new religion founded through divine revelation to Nakayama Miki. Originally classified under Sect Shinto but claims independent identity. Approximately 2 million followers, primarily in Japan. Headquarters in Tenri City, Nara Prefecture.

traditions.name: Oomoto

keyFigures.name: Deguchi Nao
keyFigures.role: Foundress, received revelations from Ushitora no Konjin

keyFigures.name: Deguchi Onisaburo
keyFigures.role: Co-founder, systematizer, spiritual leader and prolific author

keyFigures.name: Deguchi Sumiko
keyFigures.role: Daughter of Nao, wife of Onisaburo, organizational leader

keyFigures.name: Deguchi Hidemaru
keyFigures.role: Third Spiritual Leader (Sandai Kyoshu)

keyConcepts: Ushitora no Konjin as supreme deity
Chinkon-kishin (spirit pacification and return to the divine)
Art as spiritual practice (Onisaburo's pottery and calligraphy)
Esperanto as tool for universal communication
traditions.evidence: Shinto-derived new religion emphasizing universal religion ideals and world peace. Heavily suppressed by Japanese government in 1921 and 1935 incidents. Influenced numerous other Japanese new religions. Approximately 170,000 followers.

traditions.name: Rastafari

keyFigures.name: Haile Selassie I
keyFigures.role: Revered as the returned Messiah (Jah Rastafari), Emperor of Ethiopia

keyFigures.name: Marcus Garvey
keyFigures.role: Intellectual forerunner, Pan-Africanist prophet figure

keyFigures.name: Leonard Howell
keyFigures.role: First preacher of Rastafari, founded Pinnacle commune

keyFigures.name: Archibald Dunkley
keyFigures.role: Early preacher of Haile Selassie's divinity

keyFigures.name: Joseph Hibbert
keyFigures.role: Early preacher, established one of the first Rasta groups

keyConcepts: Haile Selassie as Jah (God incarnate) or chosen king
Zion (Africa, especially Ethiopia, as promised land)
Dreadlocks as Nazirite vow and covenant sign
traditions.evidence: Afrocentric spiritual movement emerging from Jamaican black empowerment traditions. Draws heavily on Ethiopian Orthodox Christian imagery and Old Testament themes. Approximately 700,000-1,000,000 adherents worldwide, spread through reggae music culture.

traditions.name: Wicca

keyFigures.name: Gerald Gardner
keyFigures.role: Founder of Gardnerian Wicca, public revelation of the craft

keyFigures.name: Doreen Valiente
keyFigures.role: High Priestess, rewrote much of the liturgy and Charge of the Goddess

keyFigures.name: Alex Sanders
keyFigures.role: Founder of Alexandrian Wicca

keyFigures.name: Starhawk (Miriam Simos)
keyFigures.role: Author of The Spiral Dance, influential feminist Wiccan

keyConcepts: The Horned God and Triple Goddess (duotheistic theology)
Wheel of the Year (eight sabbats: Samhain, Yule, Imbolc, Ostara, Beltane, Litha, Lughnasadh, Mabon)
The Wiccan Rede ('An it harm none, do what ye will')
Magic circle casting and elemental quarters
traditions.evidence: Modern pagan witchcraft religion drawing on Western esoteric traditions, folk magic, and reimagined pre-Christian European practices. Founded by Gerald Gardner who published Witchcraft Today in 1954. Estimated 1-2 million practitioners worldwide.

traditions.name: Scientology

keyFigures.name: L. Ron Hubbard
keyFigures.role: Founder, author of Dianetics and Scientology scripture

keyFigures.name: David Miscavige
keyFigures.role: Current leader, Chairman of the Board of Religious Technology Center

keyFigures.name: Mary Sue Hubbard
keyFigures.role: Co-developer of organizational structure, head of Guardian's Office

keyConcepts: Engrams (stored traumatic memories blocking spiritual progress)
The Bridge to Total Freedom (graduated spiritual path)
traditions.evidence: New religious movement founded by science fiction author L. Ron Hubbard. Dianetics published 1950; Church of Scientology incorporated 1954. Controversial religious status debated globally. Claims approximately 10 million members, independent estimates far lower.

traditions.name: Unification Church
traditions.originDateLabel: Founded 1954 as Holy Spirit Association for the Unification of World Christianity

keyFigures.name: Sun Myung Moon
keyFigures.role: Founder, regarded as True Parent and returning Messiah

keyFigures.name: Hak Ja Han
keyFigures.role: Co-founder, True Mother, current spiritual leader

keyFigures.name: Hyo Jin Moon
keyFigures.role: Eldest son, heir to leadership

keyFigures.name: Hyung Jin Moon
keyFigures.role: Seventh son, self-proclaimed successor, leads Sanctuary Church splinter

keyConcepts: True Parents (Moon and Han as sinless parents of humanity)
Blessing ceremony (mass weddings for spiritual rebirth)
Fall of Man through illicit love (not fruit)
Unification of world Christianity and all religions
traditions.evidence: Korean new religious movement founded by Sun Myung Moon in 1954, blending Christian messianism with East Asian philosophy. Known for mass wedding ceremonies. Estimated several hundred thousand active members, with extensive business and media holdings.

traditions.name: Nation of Islam

keyFigures.name: Wallace Fard Muhammad
keyFigures.role: Mysterious founder, regarded as God incarnate by adherents

keyFigures.name: Elijah Muhammad
keyFigures.role: Leader 1934-1975, established NOI theology and organizational structure

keyFigures.name: Malcolm X
keyFigures.role: Prominent minister, later broke with NOI over orthodox Islam

keyFigures.name: Louis Farrakhan
keyFigures.role: Current leader, revived NOI in 1978 after W.D. Muhammad's reforms

keyFigures.name: Warith Deen Mohammed
keyFigures.role: Son of Elijah, reformed movement toward Sunni Islam in 1975

keyConcepts: Wallace Fard Muhammad as Allah incarnate
Yakub narrative (origin of races through selective breeding)
traditions.evidence: African American Islamic new religious movement with distinctive theology differing significantly from mainstream Islam. Founded by Wallace Fard Muhammad, systematized by Elijah Muhammad. Estimated 20,000-50,000 members. Considered heterodox by mainstream Islamic scholarship.

traditions.name: Falun Gong (Falun Dafa)

keyFigures.name: Li Hongzhi
keyFigures.role: Founder and sole Master, author of Zhuan Falun

keyFigures.name: Li Hongzhi (Master Li)
keyFigures.role: Considered living Buddha/Savior figure by practitioners

keyConcepts: Elimination of karma through suffering and practice
Falun (law wheel) in the lower abdomen
Opposition to the Chinese Communist Party's persecution
traditions.evidence: Spiritual practice combining qigong exercises with moral philosophy rooted in Buddhist and Taoist traditions. Introduced by Li Hongzhi in 1992, grew to an estimated 70 million practitioners in China by 1999, then severely persecuted by the Chinese government. Active worldwide diaspora community.

traditions.name: Soka Gakkai

keyFigures.name: Tsunesaburo Makiguchi
keyFigures.role: Founder and first president, educator and value-creation philosopher

keyFigures.name: Josei Toda
keyFigures.role: Second president, rebuilt organization after WWII

keyFigures.name: Daisaku Ikeda
keyFigures.role: Third president, international expansion leader, peace activist and author

keyFigures.name: Nichiren
keyFigures.role: 13th-century Buddhist monk whose teachings form doctrinal basis

keyConcepts: Nam-myoho-renge-kyo (chanting the title of the Lotus Sutra)
Value creation (soka) through education and practice
The Gohonzon (object of devotion, mandala inscribed by Nichiren)
Buddhism for daily life and social engagement
traditions.evidence: Lay Buddhist movement based on Nichiren Buddhism. Founded in 1930 by Makiguchi, dramatically expanded by Ikeda internationally. Separated from Nichiren Shoshu priesthood in 1991. Approximately 12 million members worldwide, with Soka Gakkai International (SGI) branches in 192 countries.

traditions.name: Heathenry / Asatru

keyFigures.name: Sveinbjorn Beinteinsson
keyFigures.role: First Allsherjargodi of Asatrúarfelagid, Icelandic farmer-poet

keyFigures.name: Hilmar Orn Hilmarsson
keyFigures.role: Current Allsherjargodi (since 2003), oversaw temple construction

keyFigures.name: Stephen McNallen
keyFigures.role: Founder of the Asatru Folk Assembly in the United States

keyFigures.name: Else Christensen
keyFigures.role: Founder of the Odinic Rite, Danish-Canadian pioneer

keyConcepts: Worship of the Aesir and Vanir (Norse gods)
Wyrd and Orlog (fate and primal law)
Frith (peace and mutual obligation within community)
traditions.evidence: Modern revival of pre-Christian Norse and Germanic religious practices. Formally recognized in Iceland in 1973. Multiple organizations worldwide with varying approaches from folkish to universalist. First Asatru temple since Viking age opened in Reykjavik in 2024.

traditions.name: Neo-Druidry

keyFigures.name: John Toland
keyFigures.role: Founded earliest modern Druid order in 1717

keyFigures.name: Iolo Morganwg (Edward Williams)
keyFigures.role: Created Gorsedd of Bards, invented many 'ancient' rituals

keyFigures.name: Ross Nichols
keyFigures.role: Founded OBOD in 1964, introduced eightfold festival calendar

keyFigures.name: Philip Carr-Gomm
keyFigures.role: Chosen Chief of OBOD 1988-2020, grew it into largest Druid order

keyConcepts: Awen (divine inspiration flowing through three rays)
Wheel of the Year (eight seasonal celebrations)
Three grades: Bard, Ovate, and Druid
Reverence for the natural world and sacred landscape
traditions.evidence: Modern revival of Celtic druidic spirituality, blending historical research with Romantic-era invention and contemporary nature-centered practice. OBOD is the largest order with over 25,000 members worldwide. Recognized as a religion in the UK since 2010.

traditions.name: Kemetic Reconstructionism

keyFigures.name: Ra Un Nefer Amen
keyFigures.role: Founded Ausar Auset Society in 1973, Pan-African Kemetic approach

keyFigures.name: Tamara Siuda
keyFigures.role: Founded Kemetic Orthodoxy (House of Netjer) in 1988, served as Nisut

keyFigures.name: Kerry Wisner
keyFigures.role: Early Kemetic practitioner and community builder

keyConcepts: Ma'at (cosmic truth, balance, justice, reciprocity, and order)
Netjeru (the gods: Ra, Isis, Osiris, Horus, Thoth, etc.)
Heka (ritual magic and spoken word power)
Living in accordance with Ma'at as ethical framework
Nisut as spiritual intermediary (in Kemetic Orthodoxy)
traditions.evidence: Modern reconstruction of ancient Egyptian religious practices. Multiple organizations include the Ausar Auset Society (1973, Pan-African approach) and Kemetic Orthodoxy (1988, temple-based). Small but growing international community, primarily online.

traditions.name: Hellenismos (Hellenic Polytheism)

keyFigures.name: Tryphon Olympios
keyFigures.role: Co-founder and leader of YSEE (Supreme Council of Ethnikoi Hellenes)

keyFigures.name: Vlassis Rassias
keyFigures.role: Prominent author and spokesperson for Hellenic polytheist revival

keyFigures.name: Emperor Julian (historical)
keyFigures.role: 4th-century emperor who attempted to restore Hellenic religion, inspirational figure

keyConcepts: Worship of the Dodekatheon (Twelve Olympian Gods)
Eusebeia (piety and reverence toward the gods)
Ritual offerings (libations, incense, animal offerings in some groups)
Reconstruction based on ancient sources (Homer, Hesiod, Plato)
traditions.evidence: Modern revival of ancient Greek polytheistic religion. YSEE formally established in 1997 in Greece after organizing from 1993. Other groups include Dodekatheon and Labrys. Gained legal recognition in Greece in 2017. Several thousand active practitioners in Greece and internationally.

traditions.name: Raelism

keyFigures.name: Claude Vorilhon (Rael)
keyFigures.role: Founder, claims contact with the Elohim extraterrestrials

keyFigures.name: Brigitte Boisselier
keyFigures.role: Head of Clonaid, Raelian cloning project

keyFigures.name: Daniel Chabot
keyFigures.role: Raelian Guide of Guides, senior organizational leader

keyConcepts: Elohim as extraterrestrial creators of humanity
Atheistic religion (no supernatural God, only advanced aliens)
Embassy for the Elohim (planned welcome center)
Cloning as path to eternal life
All religious prophets were Elohim-human hybrids
traditions.evidence: UFO-based new religious movement founded by French motorsports journalist Claude Vorilhon after claimed extraterrestrial contact in 1973. Published foundational text in 1974. Claims approximately 100,000 members in over 100 countries. Notable for Clonaid human cloning claims.

connections.evidence: Joseph Smith claimed angelic revelation restoring original Christianity, incorporating Christian scripture alongside new texts
Bible Student movement emerged from Protestant Christianity with distinct non-Trinitarian interpretation
Mirza Ghulam Ahmad claimed to fulfill Islamic eschatological prophecies within Islamic framework
Emerged from Japanese religious context, originally classified as Sect Shinto though claims independent divine origin
Deguchi Nao's revelations from Ushitora no Konjin emerged from Shinto spiritual tradition
Rastafari draws heavily from Ethiopian Orthodox Christianity, Old Testament symbolism, and Afrocentric biblical interpretation
Rastafari theology incorporates strong Old Testament themes including the Nazirite vow, the Ark of the Covenant, Zion, and Israelite identity
Gerald Gardner drew on Celtic seasonal calendar and mythology alongside Western esoteric traditions
Wiccan deity concepts and some ritual elements draw on Germanic pagan symbolism
Sun Myung Moon claimed to complete Christ's unfinished mission, developing a new messianic theology rooted in Christianity
Nation of Islam uses Islamic terminology and claims Islamic identity but with heterodox theology significantly divergent from mainstream Islam
Falun Gong incorporates Buddhist concepts of karma, cultivation, and the Dharma wheel alongside Taoist qigong
Falun Gong qigong exercises and Taoist energy cultivation concepts are central to practice
Soka Gakkai is rooted in Nichiren Buddhism, specifically devotion to the Lotus Sutra
Direct reconstruction of pre-Christian Norse and Germanic religious practices based on Eddas, sagas, and archaeological evidence
Neo-Druidry revives and reimagines Celtic druidic spiritual practices, though heavily filtered through 18th-century Romanticism
Direct reconstruction of ancient Egyptian religious worship, ritual, and ethical framework based on Egyptological scholarship
Systematic revival of ancient Hellenic polytheistic worship based on ancient literary and archaeological sources
Tenrikyo as an earlier Japanese new religion helped establish patterns that influenced Oomoto's development

roles.mormonism: Founding prophet, seer, and revelator; translator of the Book of Mormon; first president of the Church

connections.detail: Claimed to restore Christ's original church through angelic visitation and priesthood restoration
Claimed to translate the Book of Abraham from Egyptian papyri, linking to Abrahamic covenant

roles.mormonism: Second president and prophet; led the Great Migration to Utah; colonizer and territorial governor

connections.detail: Succeeded Joseph Smith as leader of the largest LDS faction after Smith's assassination
Led his people through wilderness to a new promised land (Utah), earning 'American Moses' comparison

roles.jehovahs_witnesses: Founder of the Bible Student movement and first president of the Watch Tower Society

connections.detail: Taught that Christ's invisible return occurred in 1874, later revised to 1914 by successors

roles.ahmadiyya: Founder, claimed to be the promised Mahdi, Messiah, and subordinate prophet within Islam

connections.detail: Claimed prophetic status subordinate to Muhammad, fulfilling Islamic eschatological promises
Claimed to be the metaphorical second coming of Jesus; taught Jesus survived crucifixion and died in Kashmir

roles.tenrikyo: Foundress (Oyasama), became the Shrine of God the Parent (Tenri-O-no-Mikoto)

roles.oomoto: Foundress, received possession-revelations from Ushitora no Konjin; authored sacred texts in automatic writing

connections.detail: Mother-in-law to Onisaburo, who married her daughter and systematized her revelations into organized religion

roles.oomoto: Co-founder and spiritual leader; systematized theology, expanded movement to over 2 million followers

connections.detail: Married Nao's daughter and transformed her small rural following into a massive religious movement

figures.name: Haile Selassie I

roles.rastafari: Central divine/messianic figure, regarded as Jah (God) incarnate or chosen king; Emperor of Ethiopia 1930-1974

aliases: Conquering Lion of the Tribe of Judah

connections.detail: Regarded by Rastafari as the returned Christ, fulfilling biblical prophecy of the Lion of Judah
Ethiopian Solomonic dynasty claims descent from King Solomon and the Queen of Sheba

roles.rastafari: Prophetic forerunner, Pan-Africanist whose teachings inspired the Rastafari movement

connections.detail: Garvey's words about looking to Africa for a crowned king were interpreted as prophecy of Selassie's coronation

roles.wicca: Founder of Gardnerian Wicca, published Witchcraft Today (1954) revealing the modern craft to the public

connections.detail: Initiated Valiente as High Priestess; she rewrote much of his ritual material into its lasting liturgical form

roles.wicca: High Priestess, poet, and principal liturgist; rewrote the Charge of the Goddess and many core Wiccan texts

connections.detail: Initiated by Gardner, then refined and elevated the literary quality of Wiccan ritual texts

roles.unification_church: Founder and True Father; regarded as the Lord of the Second Advent completing Jesus's unfinished mission

connections.detail: Claimed divine mission to complete what Christ could not accomplish due to crucifixion — establishing the true family

roles.nation_of_islam: Messenger of Allah (Wallace Fard Muhammad); leader 1934-1975, established NOI theology and institutions

connections.detail: Claimed messenger status comparable to Prophet Muhammad, conveying the teachings of Wallace Fard Muhammad
Mentored Malcolm X who became the most prominent NOI spokesman before their break

roles.nation_of_islam: Minister and national spokesman; dramatically expanded membership through charismatic preaching
roles.islam: After Hajj pilgrimage, embraced orthodox Sunni Islam and rejected NOI racial theology

connections.detail: Mentored by Elijah Muhammad within NOI, later broke with him over theological and personal disagreements
After Hajj, embraced Prophet Muhammad's universal message, rejected NOI racial theology

roles.falun_gong: Founder and sole Master of Falun Dafa; author of Zhuan Falun and all Falun Gong teachings

connections.detail: Some practitioners regard Li as a Buddha-level being; Falun Gong uses the Dharma wheel symbolism

roles.soka_gakkai: Third president, transformed SGI into a global Buddhist movement; prolific author, peace activist, and educator

connections.detail: Soka Gakkai traces its doctrinal lineage to Nichiren through the Nichiren Shoshu priestly tradition
Nichiren Buddhism traces its ultimate lineage to Shakyamuni Buddha via the Lotus Sutra

roles.heathenry_asatru: First Allsherjargodi (high priest) of Asatrúarfelagid; farmer-poet who led successful effort for official recognition in Iceland

roles.druidry: Founded OBOD in 1964, introduced the eightfold wheel of the year and three-grade initiatory system to modern Druidry

connections.detail: Close friend of Gerald Gardner; both influenced each other's development of seasonal ritual calendars

roles.raelism: Founder and spiritual leader; claims to be the 40th and final prophet sent by the Elohim extraterrestrials

connections.detail: Raelian theology considers Jesus one of 40 Elohim-sent prophets; Rael claims to be the last in this line
Raelian theology considers Muhammad one of the prophets sent by the Elohim
Raelian theology considers the Buddha one of the prophets sent by the Elohim
